coredata.dc:title: Non-covalent decoration of carbon dots with folic acid via a polymer-assisted strategy for fast and targeted cancer cell fluorescence imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Lei, Dan
Yang, Wen
Gong, Yunqian
Jing, Jing
Nie, Hailiang
Yu, Bin
Zhang, Xiaoling
coredata.dc:description: An efficient approach for targeting and detecting folate-receptor (FR)-positive cancer cells was developed through non-covalent conjugation of folic acid (FA) on polyethyleneimine modified CDs (PEI-CDs). The fluorescent CDs were prepared by a facile hydrothermal method, and their surfaces were wrapped with positively charged PEI for further conjugation with FA through electrostatic interaction. The FA targeted PEI modified CDs (FA-PEI-CDs) can be used as a turn-on fluorescent nanoprobe for folate receptor (FR)-positive cancer cells in vivo and in vitro. The uptake of the designed FA-PEI-CDs by HeLa and HepG2 cancer cells was verified by confocal laser scanning microscopy after 10min incubation and competition experiments, as well as a comparative study with FR-negative MCF-7 cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Non-covalent decoration
Carbon dots
Folate acid
Folate receptor-positive cancer cells
Turn on fluorescent nanoprobe

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Nature Science Foundation of China (No. 21275018, 21575015, 21505004 and 21203008), Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences (BNLMS) (20140121), Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education of China (RFDP) (No. 20121101110049) and the 111 Project (B07012) for financial support. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the formation of FA-PEI-CDs, and the fluorescence turn-on probe for imaging FR+ cancer cells.
TEM images of the CDs (A), PEI-CDs (C) and FA-PEI-CDs (D), and particle size distribution histograms of CDs (B).

(A) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence (λex=300nm) emission spectra of the PEI-CDs. The inset shows photographs of PEI-CDs dispersed in water taken under daylight (left) and UV lamp (right) (λex=365nm). (B) The fluorescence emission spectra for different excitation wavelengths from 290nm to 370nm.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of PEI-CDs upon addition of different concentrations of FA. (B) Stern-Volmer plots (F 0/F) showing FA concentration dependence of the fluorescence intensity of the PEI-CDs within the range of 0–200μM. (C) Fluorescence intensity of FA-PEI-CDs after adding 0.15μg/mL FR and incubation at 37°C with reaction times of 0min, 5min, 10min, 20min, 30min, 60min, 120min. (D) Fluorescence spectra of FA-PEI-CDs in the presence of FR after incubation at 37°C for 2h. The inset: FR concentration-dependent fluorescence intensity plot showing a linear fit with a correlation coefficient R =0.994 (λ em =385nm).

Selectivity of the FA assembled PEI-CDs in 0.01M PBS toward different substances. 0, only the CDs; 1–5, the CDs plus Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, and Zn2+ (2.0mM), respectively; 6–10, the CDs plus glucose, oxalic acid, urea, ascorbic acid, and citric acid (0.4mM), respectively; 11–16, the CDs plus histidine, leucine, isoleucine, lysine, methionine, and threonine (2.0mM), respectively;17,the CDs plus FR(0.15μg/mL).
Confocal fluorescence microscopy images (A–C) and their corresponding bright-field transmission images (D–F) of HeLa, HepG2 and MCF-7 cells incubated with FA-PEI-CDs at 37°C for 10min.

head.ce:title: Non-covalent decoration of carbon dots with folic acid via a polymer-assisted strategy for fast and targeted cancer cell fluorescence imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Cluster Science of Ministry of Education, Beijing Key Laboratory of Photoelectronic/Electrophotonic Conversion Materials, School of Chemistry, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing 100081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Cluster Science of Ministry of Education
Beijing Key Laboratory of Photoelectronic/Electrophotonic Conversion Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the formation of FA-PEI-CDs, and the fluorescence turn-on probe for imaging FR+ cancer cells.

An efficient approach for targeting and detecting folate-receptor (FR)-positive cancer cells was developed through non-covalent conjugation of folic acid (FA) on polyethyleneimine modified CDs (PEI-CDs). The fluorescent CDs were prepared by a facile hydrothermal method, and their surfaces were wrapped with positively charged PEI for further conjugation with FA through electrostatic interaction. The FA targeted PEI modified CDs (FA-PEI-CDs) can be used as a turn-on fluorescent nanoprobe for folate receptor (FR)-positive cancer cells in vivo and in vitro. The uptake of the designed FA-PEI-CDs by HeLa and HepG2 cancer cells was verified by confocal laser scanning microscopy after 10min incubation and competition experiments, as well as a comparative study with FR-negative MCF-7 cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectroscopy is emerging as a powerful analytical tool for biomolecule detection, non-invasive single-cell analysis and biomedical imaging [1,2]. Compared with conventional techniques including computed tomography (CT), position–emission tomography (PET), and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), fluorescence molecular microscopy achieves depth resolutions of up to 800μm, yields three-dimensional information from light emitted by differentially labelled fluorescent objects (detected through multiple channels and from second harmonic signals), and provides hours of imaging at high spatial resolutions [3,4]. Currently, molecular imaging using fluorescence-based techniques is widely used in cancer cell detection due to its extraordinary sensitivity and spatial resolution. Fluorescence imaging visualizes the expression and activity of particular molecules, as well as cells and biological processes that influence tumor behavior and/or response to therapeutic drugs [5].

Cancer biomarkers are increasingly being adopted for in vivo cancer fluorescence targeting and imaging, because they exhibit sufficient predictive value and analytic performance, and provide essential information for the management of cancer [6–8]. Among these biomarkers, folate receptor (FR) is known to be overexpressed on the surface of several cancer cells, including malignancies of the ovary, brain, kidney, breast, and lung [9]. For FR, folic acid (FA) serves as an ideal ligand for specific labelling of FR-positive (FR+) cancer cells owing to its high affinity for FR, and its compatibility with both organic and aqueous solvents [10,11]. Moreover, the FA is readily internalized into cells through receptor mediated endocytosis with non-immunogenicity [12,13].

The conjugation of FA on fluorescent nanomaterials is a key strategy for developing applications as a probe in targeting and imaging cancer cells [14]. Numerous studies have been devoted to covalent conjugation of FA onto fluorescent nanomaterials, such as quantum dots (QD) [5,9]. The preparation of FA-conjugated fluorescent nanomaterials like QDs involves a laborious multi-step process that includes colloidal QDs synthesis, QDs solubilization, and FA functionalization [15,16]. Nevertheless, these time- and labor-consuming process, do not permit sufficient number of FA molecules to be anchored onto the surface of fluorescent nanomaterials for the following reasons: (1) there are limited attachment sites available on the fluorescent nanomaterials surface; (2) multi-functionalization steps for covalent conjugation of FA on fluorescent nanomaterials may negatively affect the fluorescent intensity of the fluorescent nanomaterials and/or the suspension stability of the particulate system [17]. Moreover, most of these FA-conjugating probes are always ON fluorescence, leading to false positives due to nonspecific endocytosis of fluorescence nanomaterial by FR+ cells [18–20]. In addition, endocytosis of FA conjugated fluorescent nanomaterials into FR+ cells is difficult in a short time period, and several hours are always needed to show obvious differences from negative controls because the surface properties of as-prepared FA-conjugated fluorescent nanomaterials may impact their interaction with FR+ cells [11,21]. For example, it is believed that cellular binding of these anionic nanoparticles is inhibited by serum proteins independent of nanoparticle composition or surface modification [22,23].

In general, the surface functionalization of fluorescent nanomaterials with various polymeric materials has been widely used for fabrication of bio-conjugated fluorescent nanomaterials [24,25]. The polymer-assisted strategies can easily generate a high surface density of functional groups for attachment and provide solubility in water [26–28]. Such strategies, however, have been devoted only to traditional fluorescent nanomaterials such as QDs. As a fascinating class of recently discovered nanocarbons, carbon dots (CDs) have proven to be powerful tools for bioimaging and biosensing because of their small size (<10nm), high quantum yields, low toxicity, good stability, and desirable biosensing properties such as high selectivity, sensitivity, and accuracy [29–32]. Moreover, recent studies demostrated that CDs can be simultaneously used as fluorescent, photoacoustic and thermal theranostics for cancer diagnosis and treatment, thus providing a new opportunity for extending the biomedical application of CDs [33–35].

To expand this methodology to carbon dots, we propose a polyethyleneimine (PEI)-assisted strategy for controllable coverage of FA ligands on CDs [36]. PEI is a cationic polymer material with repeat units composed of the amine group and two methylene spacers (CH2CH2 ) (As shown in Fig. S1), which facilitate attachment of functional moieties on CDs via non-covalent adsorption or wrapping [16]. More importantly, the high density of amino groups not only renders the CDs with good colloidal stability, but also creates uniformly distributed adsorption sites for a high density of anionic FA [37,38].

In this present study, we developed a turn-on fluorescence probe based on stable FA-modified CDs for in vivo targeting and imaging of FR+ cancer cells. In our approach, hydrothermally synthesized CDs were first conjugated with PEI via a non-covalent functionalization technique or wrapping, and then modified with FA through electrostatic interaction. The positively-charged multifunctional particles were characterized via zeta potential measurements, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and atomic force microscopy (AFM). The FA-PEI-CDs were used as a fluorescence nanoprobe for imaging of FR+ HeLa and HepG2 cells in vivo by confocal microscopy.

All chemicals used were of at least analytical grade. Na2HPO4·12H2O, NaH2PO4·2H2O, HCl, NaOH, KCl, and NaCl were from Beijing Chemical Works (Beijing, China). PEI (branched, MW 10000, 99%), ATP and FA were from Acros Organics (Beijing, China). All amino acids, ascorbic acid, glucose, citric acid, and oxalic acid were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., (Beijing, China). Dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cut off of 3500 was from Dingguo Biotech Co., Ltd., (Beijing, China). Folate receptor 2 (FR2, human) as a typical FR were from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM), trypsin, and fetal bovine serum (FBS) of Invitrogen were purchased from Life Tech. Co. (Carlsbad, CA, USA). The phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (0.01M) contained 8.0g of NaCl, 0.2g of KCl, 3.63g of Na2HPO4·12H2O, and 0.24g of KH2PO4 in 1L ultrapure water and was adjusted to a desired pH for direct use.

The morphology of dialyzed CDs was characterized on a JSM −1200EX II transmission electron microscope (TEM) operating at 200kV acceleratingvoltage (JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). UV vis absorption spectra are obtained on a PE-Lambda 35 UV–vis spectrophotometer with a quartz cuvette (path length=1cm). The pH measurements are made with a Sartorius basic pH-meter PB-10. UV-visible spectra were obtained on a Beijing Purkinje TU-1901. Fluorescence (FL) experiments were performed on a FL-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). In all the tests of steady-state fluorescence, λ ex of 300nm, delay of 0s, bandwidth (ex/em) of 5nm/5nm, PMT voltage of 700V, and response of 0.5s were adopted. Determination of the ζ potential in PBS solution at pH 7.4 was performed on a Malvern Zetasizer 3000HSa. Fluorescence microscopy images were acquired using a Zeiss710 confocal fluorescence microscope.

We use a simple method to prepare FA-PEI-CDs. First, 0.5g of ATP was added to 10mL distilled water to form a clear solution. Then, the mixture was placed in a high pressure nitrifying pot (25mL) and heated at 300°C in a Nabertherm Muffle furnace (Germany) for 4h to form the CDs. Third, 30μL of PEI was mixed with 1mL of the raw CDs solution and 4mL distilled water, and stirred for 3h at 60°C. The color of the solution was light yellow. Finally, FA and PEI-CDs solutions were mixed at the desired concentration. All solutions were prepared immediately before use.

HeLa, HepG2 and MCF-7 cells used in this study were cultured in DMEM containing 10% FBS and 1% penicillin–streptomycin at 37°C in a 5% CO2 incubator.The cells were transferred to a confocal dish, cultured for 24h, washed with 5mL of PBS (0.01M, pH 7.4) twice to ensure no dead cells. Before use, the adherent cells were washed three times with PBS (0.01M, pH 7.4). For imaging, the cells were incubated 10min with FA-PEI-CDs at 37°C, while being observed under a Zeiss710 confocal fluorescence microscope with excitation wavelength fixed at 410nm and fluorescence wavelengths at 450–600nm. In the negative control experiment, the cells were incubated with PEI-CDs but without FA for 10min.

The fluorescence turn-on probe is based on preparation of FA-PEI-CDs via a polymer-assisted strategy for imaging FR+ cancer cells (Scheme 1 ). First, the CDs were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method. Second, PEI-CDs complexes were formed by a non-covalent functionalization technique between negatively charged CDs and positively charged PEI. The PEI-CDs were then conjugated to FA by electrostatic interaction [19]. The primary fluorescence of PEI-CDs turns off when the FA is conjugated because of electron transfer to produce a negligible fluorescence background. Competitive binding to FR expressed on the surface of cancer cells enables FA desorption from PEI-CDs, thus inhibiting electron transfer between FA and PEI-CDs, and recovering the primary fluorescence of PEI-CDs [19].

The size and morphology of CDs, PEI-CDs, and FA-PEI-CDs were investigated using a transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as shown in Fig. 1 . The CDs were well-dispersed with diameters mainly distributed in the range of 1.5–3.0nm with an average diameter of 2.5nm. After electrostatic interaction of PEI onto CDs, the complexes began to form linear chain-like aggregates, and no mono-dispersed CDs were observed (Fig. 1C), indicating the successful non-covalent adsorption or wrapping. As shown in Fig. 1D, with FA modification, the aggregates were dispersed. Additionally, dynamic light scattering analysis demonstrated that the hydrodynamic size (164∼220nm) of FA-PEI-CDs is smaller than that (255∼458nm) of PEI-CDs (Fig. S2), further confirming the dispersion of PEI-CDs aggregates by FA modification. The binding of FA with PEI-CDs was verified by zeta potential measurements (−10.2mV for CDs, +8.39mV for PEI-CDs, and +3.16mV for FA–PEI-CDs). At pH 7.4 in PBS, the NH2 groups in PEI(pK b =3.0–5.0) are protonated and the COOH groups in FA (pK a =3.55) are dissociated, and the −NH3 + of PEI and the −COO− of FA interact electrostatically [16].

To further confirm the FA has been successfully immobilized, the AFM images of CDs, PEI-CDs, and FA-PEI-CDs were obtained (as shown in Fig. S3). The cross section revealed CDs with a topographic height of around 0.5nm, while the topographic heights of PEI-CDs and FA-PEI-CDs were 1.0 and 8nm, respectively. The height of FA-PEI-CDs showed significant change, indicating FA was assembled on the surface of PEI-CDs. As shown in Fig. S4, the PEI-CDs treated with FA presented similar absorption spectra as FA, further confirming the immobilization of FA on the PEI-CDs.

To explore the optical properties, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of the PEI-CDs are shown in Fig. 2 A. The absorption at 280nm can be ascribed to the π–π* electron transition of the conjugated CC band and the n–π* electron transition of the CO band [39,40]. The fluorescence emission spectrum shows a maximum emission peak at 385nm. The inset shows photographs of PEI-CD solutions, which looks light brown in daylight and a bright blue under a 365nm UV lamp. As shown in Fig. 2B, the excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior of PEI-CDs is similar to that of previous fluorescent carbon materials [21]. The emission maximum is gradually red-shifted as the excitation wavelength increases, with most intense emission (at 385nm) when the excitation wavelength is 300nm. It is believed that this phenomenon could be attributed to emissive traps [41–43] and electronic conjugate structure [44].

For targeted in vitro imaging studies, PEI-CDs were coupled to FA by electrostatic interaction. FA, a typical cell-targeting agent, has high affinity to FR which is known to be overexpressed by many human cancer cells but rarely expressed in normal cells [45,46]. Initially, we explored the feasibility of using FA to quenching the fluorescence of PEI-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3 A, upon the addition of FA, gradual FL quenching is observed using 300nm excitation, indicating that the FL intensity of PEI-CDs is sensitive to the presence of FA. Fig. 3B shows that the quenched fluorescence intensity is dependent on the FA concentration at 385nm. To understand the mechanism of the fluorescence quenching of PEI-CDs by FA, the absorption and the fluorescence emission spectra of PEI-CDs and FA were studied. Because there is no overlap between the fluorescence emission of PEI-CDs and the absorbance of FA (Fig. 2A; Fig. S5 in the Supporting information), fluorescence resonance energy transfer from PEI-CDs to FA is excluded. As surface-functionalized CDs are both excellent electron donors and electron acceptors, the fluorescence of the CDs can be quenched by electron acceptors or donors [47].

The Stern–Volmer equation is
(1)
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
C
where K SV is the Stern–Volmer constant, C represents the concentration of the added FA, F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of FA, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3B, the quenching efficiency (F 0/F) displays an upward curve when plotted versus the concentration of FA in the range of 0–200μM and does not follow equation 1, indicating that both dynamic quenching and static quenching are operative [48].

Based on the high affinity of FA to FR, FA shows stronger binding with FR than PEI on the surface of CDs. As a result of the presence of FR, FA desorbs from the surface of PEI-CDs and binds with FR, leading to the recovery of the fluorescence of the PEI-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence intensity rapidly increased to original 2.5 times in 10min incubation at 37°C, and then gradually reached a stable level after 2h. Next, the sensitivity of the FA-PEI-CDs toward FR was examined (Fig. 3D). After incubation for 2h at 37°C, the fluorescence intensity showed a progressively increase with addition of various concentrations of FR. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity at 385nm fit a good linear relationship relative to the FR concentration in the range of 0–0.15μg/mL with a detection limit of 0.5ng/mL. Considering that two hours needed to be incubated with living cells is very time-consuming, we also investigated the respond of the FA-PEI-CDs to diverse concentrations of FR in 10min incubation time (Fig. S6). The result showed that the FA-PEI-CDs have a great potential for fast imaging of tumor cells.

To investigate the fluorescence stability, FL intensity of the PEI-CDs was studied as a function of pH, ionic strength, and storage time. Fig. S7A shows the FL intensity of PEI-CDs at different pH values. The PEI-CDs gave maximal emission at pH 7.4 and had strong FL activities in the range of pH 5–12, showing that the PEI-CDs exhibit stable fluorescence even under extreme pH conditions [27]. We also explored the effect of adding different concentrations of NaCl to the PEI-CDs solutions in PBS (0.01M, pH 7.4) (Fig. S7B), and the fluorescence intensity showed no obvious change with increasing ionic strength. Moreover, we studied the influence of the storage time in Fig. S7C. The fluorescence of PEI-CDs in PBS (0.01M, pH 7.4) did not decrease obviously after 10days storage at ambient temperature in room light. In addition, Fig. S7D shows the temporal change of the quenched fluorescence intensity of the PEI-CDs in the presence of 2.0μM and 20μM FA monitored for 30min. The rapid and stable quenched FL intensity was observed immediately after the addiction of FA to PEI-CDs. These findings indicated that PEI-CDs had superior stability even under extreme conditions.

To investigate the selectivity of the FA-PEI-CDs probe for FR, we carried out the fluorescence response of the probe toward various substances, such as Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, and Zn2+ (2.0mM); glucose, oxalic acid, urea, ascorbic acid, and citric acid (0.4mM); histidine, leucine, isoleucine, lysine, methionine, and threonine (2.0mM). As shown in Fig. 4 , only FR could enhance the fluorescence of the probe, no obvious fluorescence recovery was observed in other samples. The above results indicate that the FA-PEI-CDs are highly selective for FR over other common biologically related substances and meet the selective requirements for bioimaging applications.

After investigating the optical properties of the FA-PEI-CDs in vitro, we further explored their ability to recognize FR-positive cancer cells and to distinguish FR-positive and FR-negative cancer cells. HeLa cells(a kind of human cervical carcinoma cell with a high expression of FR, FR-positive), HepG2 cells (a human liver carcinoma cell line also with a high expression of FR, FR-positive) and MCF-7 cells (a human breast adenocarcinoma cell, FR-negative) were incubated with FA-PEI-CDs for 10min at 37°C. As shown in Fig. 5 , the HeLa and HepG2 cells which are FR-positive show bright blue fluorescence, while the FR negative MCF-7 cells show no obvious fluorescence. As a negative control, these cells were incubated with PEI-CDs but without FA (Fig. S8), all thesecells show bright blue fluorescence and there is no obvious difference between different cells. All these results indicated that the as-synthesized FA-PEI-CDs can serve as an effective turn-on probe for FR+ cells.

To sum up, we have developed an efficient approach for targeting and imaging FR+ cancer cells through non-covalent modification of FA on PEI-CDs via a PEI—assisted strategy. In our approach, as-synthesized CDs were conjugated with PEI and then modified with FA through electrostatic interaction. The fluorescence of PEI-CDs turned off first upon the electrostatic adsorption of FA onto PEI-CDs based on electron transfer. The presence of FR made FA desorb from PEI-CDs due to specific and high affinity of FA to FR. The proposed turn-on probe demonstrated effective targeted imaging of FR+ HeLa and HepG2 cancer cells after a 10min incubation period. Receptor-mediated endocytosis was confirmed by competition experiments as well as a comparative study with FR-negative MCF-7 cells. Compared with the FA-modified CDs via covalent conjugation, our non-covalent FA-PEI-CDs show much simplicity and convenience in the synthesis process, reduce the membrane-penetration time from several hours to 10min in the imaging of cancer cells, and exhibit fluorescence “turn-on” response toward cancer cells. We believe these FA-PEI-CDs for targeting and detecting FR+ cancer cells would provide exciting opportunities for biomedical imaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Nature Science Foundation of China (No. 21275018, 21575015, 21505004 and 21203008), Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences (BNLMS) (20140121), Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education of China (RFDP) (No. 20121101110049) and the 111 Project (B07012) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.02.123.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Imaging in the era of molecular oncology
Photoluminescence properties of graphene versus other carbon nanomaterials
Distinguishing folate-receptor-positive cells from folate-receptor-negative cells using a fluorescence off-on nanoprobe
Folate receptor-targeted aggregation-enhanced near-ir emitting silica nanoprobe for one-photon in vivo and two-photon ex vivo fluorescence bioimaging
Targeted quantum dots fluorescence probes functionalized with aptamer and peptide for transferrin receptor on tumor cells
Molecular markers for breast cancer diagnosis, prognosis and targeted therapy
Dual-mode probe based on mesoporous silica coated gold nanorods for targeting cancer cells
Conjugating folic acid to gold nanoparticles through glutathione for targeting and detecting cancer cells
Folic acid-functionalized fluorescent gold nanoclusters with polymers as linkers for cancer cell imaging
Distinguish cancer cells based on targeting turn-on fluorescence imaging by folate functionalized green emitting carbon dots
Folate-mediated targeting: from diagnostics to drug and gene delivery
Targeting of porous hybrid silica nanoparticles to cancer cells
Folate-receptor-mediated delivery of InP quantum dots for bioimaging using confocal and two-photon microscopy
Gamma-cyclodextrin-folate complex-functionalized quantum dots for tumor-targeting and site-specific labeling
Effect of surface properties on nanoparticle–cell interactions
Spectroscopic probes with changeable pi-conjugated systems
A graphene oxide-based fluorescent biosensor for the analysis of peptide-receptor interactions and imaging in somatostatin receptor subtype 2 overexpressed tumor cells
Dual-targeted nanocarrier based on cell surface receptor and intracellular mrna: an effective strategy for cancer cell imaging and therapy
Non-specific cellular uptake of surface-functionalized quantum dots
The role of surface charge on the uptake and biocompatibility of hydroxyapatite nanoparticles with osteoblast cells
Surface Charge Affects Cellular Uptake and Intracellular Trafficking of Chitosan-Based Nanoparticles
In vivo cancer targeting and imaging with semiconductor quantum dots
Polymer coating of quantum dots—a powerful tool toward diagnostics and sensorics
Polyethylenimine-enhanced electrocatalytic reduction of co2 to formate at nitrogen-doped carbon nanomaterials
Design strategies for fluorescent biodegradable polymeric biomaterials
Graphene quantum dots: emergent nanolights for bioimaging, sensors, catalysis and photovoltaic devices
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles: synthesis, characterization, and bioimaging application
Deep ultraviolet photoluminescence of water-soluble self-passivated graphene quantum dots
Functional surface engineering of c-dots for fluorescent biosensing and in vivo bioimaging
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
A graphene quantum dot photodynamic therapy agent with high singlet oxygen generation
Red-emissive carbon dots for fluorescent, photoacoustic, and thermal theranostics in living mice
Polyethyleneimine functionalized single-walled carbon nanotubes as a substrate for neuronal growth
Self-assembly of folate onto polyethyleneimine-coated CdS/ZnS quantum dots for targeted turn-on fluorescence imaging of folate receptor overexpressed cancer cells
Decoration of a bio-inspired carbon nanosphere with Pt nanoparticles via a polymer-assisted strategy for enhanced electrocatalytic activity
Fluorescent carbon nanodots conjugated with folic acid for distinguishing folate-receptor-positive cancer cells from normal cells
Histidine-derived nontoxic nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensing and bioinnaging applications
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
An electrochemical avenue to green-luminescent graphene quantum dots as potential electron-acceptors for photovoltaics
Extraction of electrochemiluminescent oxidized carbon quantum dots from activated carbon
Nitrogen-doped carbon-rich, highly photoluminescent carbon dots from ammonium citrate
Folate receptor expression in carcinomas and normal tissues determined by a quantitative radioligand binding assay
Synthesis and grafting of thioctic acid-PEG-folate conjugates onto Au nanoparticles for selective targeting of folate receptor-positive tumor cells
Electron transfer quenching by nitroxide radicals of the fluorescence of carbon dots

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dan Lei is graduate student in Beijing Institute of Technology, China. Her current research focuses on the design and synthesis of CDs fluorescent probes.

Wen Yang received his Ph.D. degree in State Key Laboratory of Chemical Analysis of Electricity, Chinese Academy of Sciences Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, China, in 2008. After post-doctoral training in Max Planck Institute of Colloids and Interfaces in Germany，he joined Beijing Institute of Technology as an associate professor in the School of Chemistry. His research interest focuses on developing novel carbon quantum dots for biological fluorescence imaging.
Yunqian Gong is Ph.D. student in Beijing Institute of Technology, China. His current research focuses on the design and synthesis of small-molecular fluorescent probes.

Jing Jing received her Ph.D. degree in Peking University, China, in 2014. She is currently a lecturer in Beijing Institute of Technology. Her current research focuses on design and synthesis of organic fluorescent probes for super-resolution fluorescence imaging.
Bin Yu is graduate student in Beijing Institute of Technology, China. His current research focuses on the design and synthesis of small-molecular fluorescent probes.
Hailiang Nie is Ph.D. student in Beijing Institute of Technology, China. His current research focuses on the design and synthesis of small-molecular fluorescent probes.
Xiaoling Zhang is a professor in the School of Chemistry, Beijing Institute of Technology, China. Her research interest focuses on molecular recognition and chemical and biological sensors.
